results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 42]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This film is a composite of Gadsden family life in 1983 and 1984 at home at their farm and in Toorak.

Firstly, children of the family on their ponies and horses at the farm are being guided by Jess Gadsden. We then see first birthday celebrations for baby Nicko, one of the Gadsden grandchildren. There is also a Christmas party in the garden of Ron and Jess Gadsden’s Toorak home in Melbourne.

This film was originally made using Super 8mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:02:14:00 baby Nicko with the birthday cake

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 3]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This reel opens with the title ‘Surfers Paradise May 1975’ written in the sand between waves. The subtitle ‘Passing Parade’ shows the filmmakers intention: life on the beach on the Gold Coast at the time. The reel begins with early morning scenes of both the beach and the duplex holiday house situated right on the sand. We see lots of surfing – board surfing, body surfing and a longboard with paddles. We also see a large jellyfish in the sand, frisbee playing, strolling along the sand in 1970s fashion flares, Gadsdens running in a group along the beach, family members fishing, a commercial fishing boat in the waves, knitting on a towel on sand, sitting on the house balcony with drinks, a barbeque on the rooftop and views along the coastline.

This film was originally made using Super 8mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:01:56:00 ice cream cart on the beach
00:13:14:00 the filmmaker himself enjoying a XXXX beer while smiling for the camera
00:13:53:00 mid-rise apartment blocks along the coast more sparsely spaced than now

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 27]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This film is the second of two that capture a family holiday to far north Queensland and the Great Barrier Reef in May, 1975.

The Gadsden family travel by yacht to visit a tropical shore, enjoy an evening barbeque, go scuba diving and bask in the sun. They then travel to the town of Kuranda by train going through some spectacular rainforest scenery on their way.

This film was originally made using Super 8mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:00:22:00 passenger yacht ‘Tiki’ anchors off an island and lets down a rowboat.
00:00:40:00 wallabies and kangaroos join the travellers for lunch
00:00:59:00 a kangaroo eating bread from its paws
00:01:31:00 old coral and shells on a beach
00:02:00:00 various efforts to break open a coconut
00:03:07:00 eating the coconut
00:03:20:00 tropical flowers, banana trees, palms and views around the landscape
00:04:12:00 lunch being set up on the beach – cutting vegetables and a makeshift barbeque plate on the sand
00:04:23:00 entering the water for snorkelling
00:04:49:00 sausages and onion rings on the barbeque plate with flames underneath
00:05:34:00 serving food from the barbeque to the travellers
00:05:57:00 the travellers picnic on the beach
00:06:18:00 sunbathing on the beach
00:07:00:00 speeding across the water
00:07:05:00 pamphlet for ‘Cairns-Kuranda’ rail trip showing the route the travellers will follow.
00:07:25:00 lovely old train
00:07:45:00 passengers boarding a train at a rail station
00:07:54:00 views from the moving train - the track below, sugar cultivation and lush hills
00:08:23:00 ‘Redlynch’ rail station with glimpses of pot plants and ferns typical of Queensland
00:09:05:00 scenic views and waving children
00:09:25:00 view of the train snaking ahead
00:10:00:00 views of the lush landscape from a higher vantage point
00:10:25:00 the train travels through tunnels and close vegetation and local onlookers standing by the tracks
00:10:36:00 train travels over a lovely old wooden bridge as it rises higher into the forest covered hills
00:10:58:00 a waterfall
00:11:30:00 looking out over fields to the sea as the train keeps moving along
00:11:44:00 train travels past labourers – probably from sugar cane fields
00:12:00:00 a sign for ‘Barron Falls’
00:12:29:00 the camera inadvertently captures a fashion moment from the 1970s – long hair in low ‘pigtails’ tied with equally long green narrow ribbons
00:12:44:00 the Gadsden travellers looking out the train window
00:12:54:00 Jess Gadsden among other sightseers hopping back onto the train from a lookout point
00:13:20:00 coming into the picturesque Kuranda rail station filled with pot plants and foliage and views around the station
00:14:16:00 sign for ‘Kuranda’
00:14:33:00 the Gadsden travellers enjoy a beer whilst at Kuranda Station

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 22]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This reel contains yacht racing at Davies Bay Yacht Club in Melbourne and at Lake Eppalock in Victoria. Footage shows the club members preparing yachts and sailing as well as the family and friends who are watching.

This film was originally made using Super 8mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:02:50:00 children gathered at a berthed yacht
00:03:08:00 “Cheeky Chic” berthed yacht
00:03:32:00 competition rescue boat speeding away from a line up of competing yachts in preparation for the race
00:07:23:00 expansive view of yachts on the water
00:09:12:00 family members watching from the bank

The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This film shows sailing activities at the Davey’s Bay Yacht Club as well as yacht racing off the Mornington Peninsula, Victoria.

This film was originally made using Super 8mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:01:05:00 yacht sailing into bay with cliff top houses in background
00:04:10:00 hoisting a yacht into the water

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 162]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This film shows scenes of sea and shoreline – and the people enjoying them – at Phillip Island, Victoria, in the late 1940s.

This film was originally made using 16mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:00:00:00 footage of the sea and coastline, with waves breaking onto a rocky outcrop
00:00:47:00 people on a headland above the sea, with a stairway down to the waterline (at ‘The Nobbies’ area of Phillip Island). The film continues with more footage of coastal features and surging seas.
00:02:47:00 people (photographed from the rear) on a rise, looking out to sea
00:03:10:00 people riding distinctive boards in the surf (boards have an upward curve at the front
00:03:50:00 a very young child building sandcastles on the beach
00:04:09:0 people sunbaking and relaxing on the beach
00:04:18:00 man and young child walking along the shoreline with a large yacht in the background
00:04:32:00 people laying on the beach – details of 1940s swimming fashions and sunglasses
00:04:58:00 people in the water, with a large steamboat in the background (this could be a vessel of the ferry service to Phillip Island, which originally began in 1878 and switched to a car-ferry service in 1933)
00:05:06:00 a young child with an adult male playing in the shallows, building (and destroying) sandcastles

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 149]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.
This very short colour film shows a family wedding in 1947.

This film was originally made using 16mm film; it is underexposed and slightly shaky.

Selected highlights list:
00:00:15:00 two bridesmaids in blue satin dresses with floral hair pieces and colourful bouquets
00:00:25:00 the bride arrives wearing a white satin gown with a full net veil; the groom wears a classic dark suit, white shirt and tie
00:01:05:00 scenes of the bridal party and quests in front of the church
00:01:28:00 guests dressed for a 1940s style wedding

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 121]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This reel shows the Gasdsen family and friends enjoying a skiing holiday at Perisher, a large ski resort in the Snowy Mountains of New South Wales. A particular highlight in the footage is a glimpse of the Sundeck Hotel which was built in 1959 and rebuilt in 1960 after a fire. The reel ends with shots of skiing, the snow covered mountains and buildings.

This film was originally made using 16mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:00:59:00 people skiing down a slope only to fall in a heap, laughing in front of the camera
00:02:38:00 strapping on skis alongside the Sundeck Hotel
00:02:56:00 skiers jumping on a ski lift and waving at the camera as they sail by; the camera follows the lift showing it rising with the snow covered slopes
00:03:11:00 a woman and boy we saw earlier, catching the ski lift and waving at the camera
00:00:00:00 vehicle with snow tyres hauling a trailer near some buildings in deep snow

results.title: [Gadsden Family Home Movie Collection: Reel 58]
results.brief_description:
The Gadsden Company has a long and proud history of manufacture, dating back to its foundation by Jabez Gadsden in 1879. The company pioneered tinplate printing in Australia, and produced the first printed can the country had seen. By 1936 the company had twelve factories spread throughout Australia and New Zealand.

The company’s success allowed two generations of Gadsden family men – Stanley Gadsden and his son Ronald – to pursue their interest in amateur filmmaking (a relatively expensive hobby for the time). The two cameramen captured the family’s children, homes, many of its major events and holidays on film.

A collection of the films made by these Gadsden family photographers has been donated to ACMI by Chris Gadsden (Ronald’s son). The films comprise a fascinating assortment of imagery that provides a window into bygone days and customs.

This film captures Gadsden family gatherings at the poolside and for a Christmas party at their Toorak, Melbourne home in the 1970s.

There are some great examples of 1970s style fashion trends and hair styles across the generations and a view into the family’s life at home, such as enjoying their backyard pool. Unusually, filmmaker Ron himself is filmed handing out the presents at the Christmas party.

This film was originally made using Super 8mm film.

Selected highlights list:
00:00:15:00 scenes of family floating the pool in the family garden
00:00:40:00 an array of fashion of the era
00:00:58:00 a beer moment
00:01:28:00 Ron Gadsden in a 1970s style cravat
00:02:46:00 Ron distributes Christmas presents
00:03:41:00 Jess Gadsden in the pool
